ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for coffee-topped bun; closest defensible match is bread roll (FOODON_03315021). Coffee-flavoured topping is not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar products; no specific code exists for coffee-topped sweet bun within the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for coffee bun; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard coffee bun weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single serving at breakfast or as a snack.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coffee Bun (CR ID F05020033). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bread Products — University of Sydney GI Database (White bread and enriched sweet bread analogues)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore-style coffee bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on the closest analogous food: enriched white bread roll / sweet bun (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database). The coffee-flavoured topping is predominantly butter and sugar and does not materially alter the glycemic response of the refined-flour base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 35.44) / 100 ≈ 24.8, rounded to 24.9 for an 80 g serving (44.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.80 = 35.44 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (473 mg/100 g) and high sugar (17.4 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit coffee bun consumption; the 473 mg sodium per 100 g approaches one-fifth of the 2000 mg daily limit in a single serving, and the high sugar content (17.4 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (17.4 g/100 g) exceeding the 15 g/100 g T2DM gate, high refined carbohydrate (44.3 g/100 g), and very low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of coffee bun; the combination of 17.4 g sugar and 44.3 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.0 g fibre produces a high glycemic load (GL ≈ 25 per serving) and rapid postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g), high sodium (473 mg/100 g), and high sugar (17.4 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit coffee bun to occasional consumption; the estimated saturated fat of approximately 11.5 g/100 g substantially exceeds the 5 g/100 g CVD threshold, and the combined sodium and sugar load further elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coffee bun is a soft, enriched bread roll popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, characterised by a distinctive coffee-flavoured butter topping that bakes into a thin, crispy, aromatic crust over a pillowy interior. The dough is made from refined wheat flour, yeast, butter, eggs, sugar, and milk, producing a rich, slightly sweet crumb. The topping — a piped mixture of butter, sugar, instant coffee, and sometimes flour — caramelises during baking to create a contrasting crunchy shell. The bun is commonly eaten at breakfast or as a mid-morning snack and is widely available at bakeries, coffee shops, and convenience stores throughout Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, coffee bun is energy-dense at 395 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 20.2 g (predominantly saturated fat estimated at ~11.5 g), high sugar at 17.4 g, and moderate protein at 9.0 g. Sodium is elevated at 473 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 1.0 g/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 25 per standard 80 g serving. These characteristics make coffee bun unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is best treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a daily staple.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a coffee bun?
Is coffee bun high in sugar?
Is coffee bun suitable for hypertension?
How much sodium in a coffee bun?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05020033) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~11.5 g/100 g based on typical butter-enriched sweet bun composition as no explicit saturated fat value was provided in the HPB record. GI estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (enriched white bread roll, GI ~70). All other -1 fields reflect genuine absence of data in the HPB source record.
notes: Saturated fat (11.5 g/100 g) is estimated from typical butter-enriched sweet bun composition; the HPB source record does not explicitly report saturated fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient panels, and moisture data for this specific coffee bun preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. GI value of 70 is an analogous estimate based on enriched white bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singapore-style coffee bun was found in any approved source. The input record contained a GI value of 0 with no source, which was rejected as unsupported. The input health_suitability entries for 'diabetes' and 'hypertension' have been remapped to 'type2_diabetes' and 'hypertension' respectively per Veritas naming rules. The rheumatoid_arthritis condition from the input has been preserved in health_context as an additional condition is not required by the scoring model; the three mandatory conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are all present. Trans fat caution noted in input RA section (0.1 g/100 g) is unconfirmed by HPB source; trans_fat_g set to -1 pending label verification.
